Player 4: I am Minion with evil teammate 0. Proposed team [1,2] contains no known evil, so if approved the quest likely passes and gives good side an early success with little sabotage opportunity. In round 1, rejecting a pure-good-looking team is generally better for evil because it avoids handing good a clean pass and may create uncertainty later. My vote itself reveals little since early votes are noisy.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil, so players 1 and 2 are the only non-seen-evil players besides me. On quest 1 with team [1,2], this is a strong good-leaning team because it excludes known evil. As good, approving a team of two unknown/non-evil players is optimal to try to secure an early success without exposing my knowledge too strongly. Approving also aligns with normal good play and does not reveal Merlin significantly.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so team [1,2] contains no known evil. As evil Assassin in round 1, I generally prefer approving teams without evil on them because they are likely to pass and give good an early success, but rejecting can create suspicion patterns too. However, on the very first vote, approves are common and a reject on a clean 2-person team can look more contrarian. My vote reveals a little; approving keeps me less conspicuous and preserves flexibility for later rounds.
Player 2: As a Servant, I have no hidden information yet. On round 1 with no prior evidence, a 2-player team including myself and the leader is a standard reasonable opener. Approving preserves information flow and avoids needlessly burning rejection rounds. My vote reveals little beyond being willing to test an uncontroversial first team, which generally helps Good.
Player 1: No role-specific hidden info as a Servant. On round 1 with no evidence, approving an initial 2-person team that includes myself is standard to gather information and avoid needless early rejects. My vote reveals little beyond willingness to test a neutral opening team, which generally helps Good more than stalling.

This is team voting on quest 3 with proposed team [1,2]. As evil, I want to prevent a likely-good team from getting another success and moving Good to match point. Rejecting is optimal. My vote itself gives limited info; an evil rejection here is standard and worth the suspicion.
Player 0: Players 1 and 2 have been on both successful quests, so they look Good to the table; as evil Assassin with teammate 4, this proposed team [1,2] excludes all evil and would likely succeed again, putting Good at 3 successes. In team voting, evil should try to block clean Good teams, especially on quest 3 where another success is dangerous.
